coredata.dc:title: Cd-doped and CTAB-modified BiOBr nanomicrospheres for efficient photocatalytic degradation of Rhodamine B
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Chu, Chengzhi
Wang, Ruihuan
Zhang, Wenwu
Chen, Xiangyu
Sun, Xiaoyi
Wang, Limin
coredata.dc:description: Human survival and development have caused serious pollution of water resources, which contain organic pollutants that pose a serious threat to human health. Although BiOBr has attracted much attention in the photocatalytic field due to its unique structure that can improve the charge separation efficiency, its lack of visible light absorption and charge separation ability limits its further application. To this end, here we present a strategy of Cd-doped BiOBr, which increases the specific surface area of BiOBr while suppressing the rate of photogenerated electron-hole complexation, thus improving its photocatalytic performance. Interestingly, when Cd was doped and CTAB was added to BiOBr (BCd1C), the optimal photocatalytic performance was obtained although the specific surface area of the catalyst was reduced. The composite photocatalyst after simultaneous doping of Cd and addition of CTAB showed a degradation rate of 99.2 % for rhodamine B in 15 min, which was much higher than that of 93.67 % for the pure BiOBr. This is because the cationic head group of CTAB adsorbs on the catalyst surface by electrostatic interaction, forming a positively charged layer that promotes the adsorption of negatively charged RhB molecules. Moreover, a series of reactive species trapping experiments revealed that the main reactive radicals catalyzing the degradation of RhB at BCd1C are ·OH and h +. The results and insights of this study provide valuable references for the further development of novel photocatalysts for the efficient degradation of dye pollutants from wastewater.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cd-doped
CTAB
Photocatalytic degradation
Rhodamine B
BiOBr

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of pure BiOBr, BCd1 and BCd1C.
SEM images of (a) pure BiOBr, (b) BCd0.5, (c) BCd1, (d) BCd2, (e) BCd1C and (f) BCd1C at magnification.
(a) TEM image of BCd1C, (b) HRTEM image of BCd1C, (c) High-angle annular dark-field scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-STEM) image of BCd1C, (d-g) EDS mapping images of BCd1C.
(a) XPS full spectrum of BCd1C. (b-e) high resolution XPS spectra of Br 3d, Bi 4f, O1s and Cd 3d of BCd1. (f) N2 adsorption-desorption diagram of BCd0, of BCd1 and of BCd1C.
(a, c) Catalytic degradation diagrams of pure BiOBr, BCd0.5, BCd1, BCd2. (b, d) kinetic curves of pure BiOBr, BCd1, and BCd1C.

(a) UV–vis DRS plot of pure BiOBr, BCd0.5, BCd1, BCd2 and BCd1C. (b) (αhν)1/2(eV)1/2 vs hν plot of pure BiOBr, BCd0.5, BCd1, BCd2 and BCd1C. (c) PL spectrogram of pure BiOBr, BCd0.5, BCd1, BCd2 and BCd1C. (d) Mott-Schottky plot of BCd1 and BCd1C.
(a) Active substance capture assay for BCd1C. (b) Schematic diagram of the photocatalytic mechanism of BCd1C.
Specific surface area, average pore diameter and pore volume of BCd0, BCd1 and BCd1C.

head.ce:title: Cd-doped and CTAB-modified BiOBr nanomicrospheres for efficient photocatalytic degradation of Rhodamine B

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: dSchool of Chemistry, Beijing Advanced Innovation Center for Biomedical Engineering, Key Laboratory of Bio-Inspired Smart Interfacial Science and Technology, Beihang University, Beijing 100191, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Human survival and development have caused serious pollution of water resources, which contain organic pollutants that pose a serious threat to human health. Although BiOBr has attracted much attention in the photocatalytic field due to its unique structure that can improve the charge separation efficiency, its lack of visible light absorption and charge separation ability limits its further application. To this end, here we present a strategy of Cd-doped BiOBr, which increases the specific surface area of BiOBr while suppressing the rate of photogenerated electron-hole complexation, thus improving its photocatalytic performance. Interestingly, when Cd was doped and CTAB was added to BiOBr (BCd1C), the optimal photocatalytic performance was obtained although the specific surface area of the catalyst was reduced. The composite photocatalyst after simultaneous doping of Cd and addition of CTAB showed a degradation rate of 99.2 % for rhodamine B in 15 min, which was much higher than that of 93.67 % for the pure BiOBr. This is because the cationic head group of CTAB adsorbs on the catalyst surface by electrostatic interaction, forming a positively charged layer that promotes the adsorption of negatively charged RhB molecules. Moreover, a series of reactive species trapping experiments revealed that the main reactive radicals catalyzing the degradation of RhB at BCd1C are ·OH and h +. The results and insights of this study provide valuable references for the further development of novel photocatalysts for the efficient degradation of dye pollutants from wastewater.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent decades, alongside the ongoing advancement of industrial technology, humanity has also been confronted with a range of environmental pollution challenges, particularly water pollution [1,2]. The current contamination of water resources is primarily driven by industrial wastewater, which contains numerous toxic substances and organic compounds. These pollutants not only inflict significant harm on aquatic ecosystems but also pose serious risks to human health [3,4]. Numerous methods have been proposed to address pollutants in wastewater, such as chemical oxidation, physical adsorption, and biodegradation [5–7]. However, these approaches are often costly and challenging to implement in practical settings. More critically, they can also generate secondary pollution during the treatment process [8]. Although electrocatalytic technology can also utilize solar energy as the energy source, photocatalysis features simple equipment, convenient operation, and the ability to trigger reactions using natural light, making it more suitable for large-scale environmental governance (e.g., pollutant degradation) [9–13]. Semiconductor photocatalysis, utilizing solar energy to drive the degradation of pollutants into non-toxic end products like H2O [14] and CO2 [15], has emerged as a promising and environmentally benign alternative. Therefore, a series of photocatalysts applied in various complex environments have been reported, such as metal oxides (TiO2 [16,17], WO3 [18], etc.), sulphides [19], bismuth-based oxides [20,21], halogen oxides (AgX [22], BiOX [23]), etc.

BiOBr, with its intricate band structure, distinctive optical properties, and a visible light-responsive bandgap of approximately 2.7 eV, has garnered significant attention in photocatalysis due to its unique layered structure that enhances charge separation efficiency [24]. However, the visible light absorption and charge separation capabilities of BiOBr are inadequate for practical applications, prompting approaches like crystal plane modulation, oxygen vacancy engineering, heterojunction construction, and noble metal deposition to enhance its photocatalytic performance [25–28]. In particular, elemental doping is also an option for improving photocatalytic performance [29–31]. Elemental doping can modify the band structure by introducing impurity energy levels, enhancing charge separation, transport efficiency, and overall catalytic performance [32]. Compared with heterostructures, doping not only avoids the potential loss of active sites at the heterojunction interface but also enables more precise tuning of the semiconductor bandgap. Cd ion doping has been shown to enhance charge separation efficiency and boost visible-light absorption capacity [33,34]. More importantly, the ionic radius of cadmium ions (Cd2+, 0.095 nm) closely matches that of Bi3+ (0.103 nm), allowing it to gently embed into the BiOBr lattice via ion exchange and form a stable solid solution without significantly altering the crystalline structure. This structural compatibility avoids excessive defect generation caused by lattice mismatch, thereby reducing carrier recombination centers. Therefore, doping cadmium ions in BiOBr is expected to improve its charge separation efficiency and thus its photocatalytic activity.

In this paper, we proposes a dual-modification strategy involving simultaneous Cd doping and CTAB surfactant modification of BiOBr, achieving exceptional photocatalytic RhB degradation performance. The composite photocatalyst demonstrated a degradation efficiency of 99.2 % for rhodamine B within 15 min. Mechanistic investigations revealed that Cd doping effectively suppresses the recombination rate of photogenerated electron-hole pairs, while CTAB modification creates a positively charged surface layer that enhances adsorption of negatively charged RhB molecules through electrostatic interactions. More importantly, systematic radical scavenging experiments confirmed that hydroxyl radicals (·OH) and photogenerated holes (h +) serve as the dominant active species responsible for RhB degradation. This work demonstrates a novel co-modification approach that opens new avenues for developing high-efficiency photocatalytic systems targeting dye pollutant removal in wastewater treatment.
The experimental precursors used in this experiment, such as Bi(NO3)3•5H2O, KBr, CdBr2•4H2O, CTAB and glycerol, were of analytical grade and did not require further purification.

To prepare pure BiOBr, 1 mmol of Bi(NO3)3•5H2O was first weighed and added to a solution consisting of 10 mL of water and 10 mL of propanetriol, and after complete dissolution, 1 mmol of KBr was added, and magnetic stirring was carried out for 30 min at room temperature, and then the stirred solution was transferred to an autoclave having 25 ml of polytetrafluoroethylene, and then it was placed in an oven at 160 °C for 6 h. When it was cooled down to room temperature, the precipitates were washed with water and ethanol repeatedly several times, respectively, in order to remove the un-reacted impurities, and finally the samples were placed in the oven at 60 °C to be dried for 24 h in order to obtain pure BiOBr.

To prepare Cd-BiOBr, KBr was replaced with CdBr2•4H2O and the rest of the steps were consistent. The molar ratio of Bi/Cd was controlled by adding different amounts of CdBr2•4H2O (1:0.5, 1:1, 1:2) to adjust the sample properties. The obtained Cd-BiOBr was named BCd0.5, BCd1 and BCd2 according to different molar ratios of Bi/Cd. The sample obtained by adding 0.05 g CTAB during the preparation of BCd1 was named BCd1C.

The photocatalytic activity of pure BiOBr, BiOBr doped with Cd and BCd1C were compared by degrading rhodamine B (RhB). A 300 W xenon lamp was used as the visible light source for irradiating the suspension, and a 420 nm filter was placed on the window surface. 10 mg of photocatalyst was added to 50 mL of 20 mg/L RhB solution and the adsorption-desorption equilibrium was established by stirring in the dark for 30 min at room temperature, and roughly 4 ml of the suspension was taken at 5 min intervals and then centrifuged using a centrifuge. The upper clear layer in the centrifuge tube was tested using an ultraviolet visible absorption spectrophotometer (UV–Vis) (maximum absorption wavelength 554 nm) to obtain the degradation rate of BiOBr on RhB. The degradation rate was calculated as:
A =
C 0
− C
C 0
×
100 %
Where C0 is the concentration of the original solution after 30 min of dark stirring; C is the concentration of the solution after t time of light.

The prepared samples were analysed for crystal structure and phase purity using X-ray diffraction (XRD). Fig. 1 shows the XRD patterns of pure BiOBr, BiOBr doped with Cd (BCd1) and BCd1C. The pure BiOBr, BCd1 and BCd1C diffraction peaks all appear at 2θ = 25.20°, 31.72°, 32.24°, 46.24° and 57.16°, respectively, due to (101), (102), (110), (200) and (212) crystal plane diffraction, in accordance with the standard card (JCPDS NO.39–0309). The diffraction peaks of BCd1 and BCd1C were weaker and less crystalline compared to pure BiOBr, but no additional peaks appeared, confirming the sample purity and that Cd doping did not alter the crystal structure of BiOBr. Additionally, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) results confirm the presence of CTAB in the BCd1C sample (Fig. S1).

Fig. 2 presents the scanning electron microscope (SEM) images of pure BiOBr, Cd-doped BiOBr with varying ratios, and CTAB-modified Cd-doped BiOBr. Pure BiOBr exhibits a microsphere structure composed of nanosheets with inter-sheet voids (Fig. 2(a)). When the Bi/Cd molar ratio was 1:0.5, the nanosheets exhibited non-uniform sizes, with some failing to aggregate into spheres and instead gathering around or attaching to the sphere surfaces (Fig. 2(b)). At a 1:1 molar ratio (Fig. 2(c)), the nanosheets became more uniform in size and formed complete microspheres. However, at a Bi/Cd molar ratio of 1:2 (Fig. 2(d)), the BiOBr microspheres began to cleave, and the nanosheets started to dissolve, resulting in incomplete spheres. A sample of BCd1C (Fig. 2(e-F)) was synthesized by adding CTAB during the preparation of BCd1. The particle size distribution of the BCd1C sample is 2.14 μm (Fig. S2). Compared to BCd1, the resulting microspheres exhibited larger pores and a looser structure. This loose microsphere structure has been shown to facilitate reactant migration and enhance electron-hole separation efficiency, thereby improving photocatalytic performance [35]. Moreover, Zeta potential results show that the addition of CTAB promotes the catalyst surface to carry a positive charge (\) [40–42].

Fig. 3 shows the morphology and elemental distribution of BCd1C. Fig. 3(a) displays the transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of BCd1C, revealing a solid sphere composed of nanosheets, consistent with the SEM observations. The lattice spacing of 0.18 nm observed in the TEM image corresponds to the (104) crystal plane of BiOBr (Fig. 3(b)). The results of EDS spectra showing the distribution of Bi, Br, O and Cd elements confirm the successful doping of Cd in BiOBr and the relatively uniform distribution of these elements (Fig. 3(c-g)).

We performed further electronic state analysis of the synthesized BCd1C by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Fig. 4(a) presents the XPS full spectrum of BCd1C and the high-resolution XPS spectra of its individual elements. The XPS full spectrum of BCd1C detects Cd, O, Bi, and Br, consistent with the EDS results, confirming the successful synthesis of BCd1C. Two fitted peaks at 68.38 eV and 67.38 eV (Fig. 4(b)) correspond to Br 3d3/2 and Br 3d5/2, respectively [36]. Two characteristic peaks at 163.58 eV and 158.28 eV (Fig. 4(c)) are assigned to the Bi 4f5/2 and Bi 4f7/2 orbitals, both indicative of Bi3+ [37]. Two fitted peaks at 531.38 eV and 529.28 eV (Fig. 4(d)) are attributed to lattice oxygen in Bi2O3 (Bi-O bond) [38]. Two main peaks at 411.38 eV (Cd 3d3/2) and 404.68 eV (Cd 3d5/2), along with a satellite peak at 401.78 eV (Fig. 4(e)), confirm the presence of Cd in the +2 valence state.

The specific surface area and pore size distribution of the BCd0, BCd1, and BCd1C samples were measured using BET analysis, as shown in Fig. 4(f) and Table 1 . All samples exhibit type IV isotherms with H3-type hysteresis loops, indicating the presence of mesoporous structures [39]. Compared to pure BiOBr, Cd-doped BiOBr exhibits a larger specific surface area of 35.798 m2 g-1. This provides more active sites for reactions, enhancing its interaction with organic compounds and improving photocatalytic performance. Interesting, after the addition of CTAB, the specific surface area of BiOBr decreased, and the pore diameter increased. This phenomenon may be attributed to the partial cleavage of the spherical structures upon CTAB addition, causing the nanosheets to adhere to each other and reducing the specific surface area. Additionally, the reduction in the number of nanosheets composing the spheres led to larger voids between the sheets, resulting in increased pore volume.

Fig. 5(a) illustrates the catalytic degradation of RhB by pure BiOBr and BiOBr with varying Cd contents. Pure BiOBr achieved a degradation efficiency of 93.67 % after 15 min. As the Bi/Cd molar ratio decreased, the catalytic degradation efficiency of BiOBr initially increased and then decreased. The highest degradation efficiency was observed at a Bi/Cd molar ratio of 1:1, reaching 96.85 % after 15 min, which is 1.25 times the photocatalytic degradation rate of pure BiOBr (Fig. 5(b)) [45–47]. However, when the amount of Cd added was twice that of Bi, the catalytic activity of the resulting BiOBr was even lower than that of pure BiOBr. Excessive introduction of Cd may introduce more defects in the catalyst, which act as recombination centers for electron-hole pairs. This leads to increased recombination of photogenerated carriers and ultimately results in catalytic performance deterioration. Fig. 5(c) shows the catalytic degradation efficiency of BiOBr after the addition of CTAB. When CTAB was added to BCd1, the photocatalytic degradation rate reached 99.2 % after 15 min, which is 1.11 times higher than that of BCd1. This indicates that the addition of CTAB enhanced its catalytic activity. The results of total organic carbon (TOC) show that the total organic carbon decreased by 17 % after degradation (Table S2). Additionally, the photocatalytic degradation performance of BCd1C was also significantly superior to that of other reported literature (Table S3). The excellent catalytic performance can be attributed to the superior charge transfer ability of the BCd1C catalyst (Fig. S3).

To further investigate the mechanism behind the enhanced intrinsic photocatalytic properties, the samples were subjected to photocurrent testing. Fig. 6(a) presents the light absorption characteristics of the samples within the 350–650 nm range, as measured by DRS. Above 450 nm, BCd1 and BCd1C exhibit significantly better light absorption compared to pure BiOBr, with the absorption edges of BCd1C notably broadened. The band gap energies of BiOBr before and after doping were calculated using the Kubelka-Munk function:
(
α h ν
)
n
= A
(
h ν −
E g
)
where: α, hν, n, Eg and A are the absorption coefficient, photon energy, leap properties of the semiconductor (direct semiconductor n = 2, indirect semiconductor n = 1/2), band gap and constant, respectively. The calculated band gap widths (Eg) for pure BiOBr and BCd1 are about 2.50 and 2.35 eV, respectively (Fig. (b)), with the Cd-doped BiOBr forbidden band width narrowing. In contrast, the band gap of BCd1C is narrower.

The photogenerated electron-hole recombination rates were characterized using PL spectroscopy, where lower peak intensities indicate reduced recombination rates and, consequently, stronger photocatalytic effects (Fig. 6(c)). The PL intensities of the samples follow the order: BCd2 > BCd0.5 > pure BiOBr > BCd1 > BCd1C. Additionally, results from transient photocurrent measurements show that the transient photocurrent density of BCd1C is higher than that recorded for BiOBr. This indicates that photoexcited charge carriers at the interface of the BCd1C catalyst exhibit higher separation efficiency, which is more favorable for the effective transfer and utilization of photogenerated electrons (Fig. S4) [43].

Fig. 6(d) displays the Mott-Schottky curves for BCd1 and BCd1C. The positive slopes of the curves confirm that both materials are n-type semiconductors. The flat band potentials (Efb) of BCd1 and BCd1C are determined to be −0.05 V and −0.30 V (vs. Ag/AgCl), respectively. The corresponding potentials in the normal hydrogen electrode scale (ENHE) can be calculated using the following equation:
E NHE
=
E
Ag / AgCl
+ 0.21

The calculated Efb values for BCd1C are −0.09 V (νs. NHE) respectively. In general, the flat-band potential of an n-type semiconductor is about 0.1 V higher than its conduction band potential (ECB), so that the conduction band potential (ECB) of BCd1C is 0.01 V (νs. NHE) respectively. The calculated ECB combined with the band gap width calculated by UV–vis DRS shows that the valence band potential (EVB) of BCd1C is 2.29 V (νs. NHE).

To better understand the involvement of various reactive radicals in the catalytic degradation of RhB by BCd1C under visible light irradiation, a series of reactive substance capture experiments were conducted. The data obtained are plotted in Fig. 7(a) . The roles of •O2 -, •OH, and h + were investigated using p-benzoquinone (BQ), isopropyl alcohol (IPA), and sodium oxalate (Na2C2O4) as the respective capture agents. The results revealed that the catalytic degradation rate of RhB by BCd1 did not change significantly after the addition of BQ compared to the system without the capture agent. However, the degradation rate decreased significantly after the addition of IPA and Na2C2O4. These findings indicate that the main reactive radicals involved in the catalytic degradation of RhB by BCd1 are •OH and h +. We conducted electron-spin resonance (ESR) experiments with DMPO as a trapping agent to further confirm that •OH is the primary reactive species for the BCd1C photocatalyst in the degradation of RhB (Fig. S5) [44].

A schematic diagram of the photocatalytic mechanism of BCd1 is presented in Fig. 7(b). Under visible light irradiation, the catalyst generates electron-hole pairs. Since the conduction band potential of the catalyst is more positive than the redox potential of O2/•O2 -, the photogenerated electrons are unable to reduce O2 to •O2 -. Meanwhile, the photogenerated holes can oxidize both H2O and surface-adsorbed OH- to produce •OH, which is the primary species responsible for the degradation and decolorization of dye molecules. Additionally, h+ can directly oxidize dye molecules to form reactive intermediates.
BiOBr + hνVisible → e - CB + h + VB (1)
h + VB + H2O → •OH + H +(2)
h + VB + OH- → •OH (3)
•OH + dye → degradation of dye molecules (4)
h + VB + dye→dye0+ → oxidation of dye molecule (5)

In conclusion, we prepared Cd-doped and CTAB-modified BiOBr nanomicrospheres by a simple solvothermal method. The degradation rate of rhodamine B by the composite photocatalyst reached 99.2 % in 15 min, which was much higher than that of 93.67 % for pure BiOBr. This is not only because Cd doping inhibits the rate of photogenerated electron-hole complexation, but also because CTAB promotes the adsorption of rhodamine B molecules through electrostatic adsorption. A series of reactive species trapping experiments revealed that the main reactive radicals catalyzing the degradation of RhB at BCd1C are -OH and h +. We believe that this result is useful for the subsequent design and preparation of more efficient photocatalysts for the degradation of organic pollutant dyes.

Chengzhi Chu: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Data curation. Ruihuan Wang: Methodology, Data curation. Wenwu Zhang: Methodology, Investigation. Xiangyu Chen: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Xiaoyi Sun: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization. Limin Wang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: This work was supported by the Postdoctoral Fellowship Program of CPSF under Grant Number GZC20242149.
Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.molstruc.2025.142960.
